content.full_text:
STATE OF CALIFORNIA
FAIR POLITICAL PRACTICES COMMISSION
428 J Street • Suite 620 • Sacramento, CA 95814-2329
(916) 322-5660 • Fax (916) 322-0886

April 27, 2017

Derek McDonald
Attorney
375 Eleventh Street
Oakland, CA 94607-4240

Re: Your Request for Advice
Our File No. A-17-045

Dear Mr. McDonald:

This letter responds to your request for advice on behalf of the East Bay Municipal Utility District regarding the conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act (the "Act").¹ Please note, the Fair Political Practices Commission does not provide advice relating to past conduct. This letter should not be construed as assistance on any conduct that may have already taken place. (See Regulation 18329(b)(8)(A).) Additionally, we are only providing advice under the conflict of interest provisions of the Act and not under other general conflict of interest prohibitions such as common law conflict of interest or Section 1090.

QUESTION

May a member of the District's Board of Directors act to appoint an immediate family member as a Successor Officer under the California Emergency Services Act when the immediate family member will be compensated if seated as a Successor Officer following an emergency?

CONCLUSION

No. A member of the District's Board of Directors may not make, participate in making, or use his or her official position to influence any decision to appoint an immediate family member as a Successor Officer.

FACTS

The East Bay Municipal Utility District (the "District") is a local government agency formed under the Municipal Utilities District Act, and provides water and wastewater services to 1.3 million customers in Alameda and Contra Costa Counties. It is governed by a seven-member elected Board of Directors from seven District wards. The California Emergency Services Act

¹ The Political Reform Act is contained in Government Code Sections 81000 through 91014. All statutory references are to the Government Code, unless otherwise indicated. The regulations of the Fair Political Practices Commission are contained in Sections 18110 through 18997 of Title 2 of the California Code of Regulations. All regulatory references are to Title 2, Division 6 of the California Code of Regulations, unless otherwise indicated.

File No. A-17-045
Page No. 2

provides for the succession of governing officers in the event an officer becomes unavailable or in
the event of a state or local emergency. It allows Regular Officers to pre-appoint up to three
successor Standby Officers to serve if the Regular Officer becomes unavailable. (See Sections 8637
and 8638.) Upon a determination that a Regular Officer is unavailable following an emergency, the
Standby Officer may serve on the governing body until he or she is dismissed, the Regular Officer
becomes available, or until the next election for Regular Officers. (See Sections 8640 and 8641(d).)

The District is developing a Board Succession Plan to address the event of a Board
Member's unavailability following a state or local emergency. Under the District's draft plan, each
Board Member is to recommend three Successor Officers to replace him or her in the event of an
unavailability resulting from an emergency. The recommended Successor Officers will be
investigated and vetted by District staff to make sure they meet the eligibility requirements of the
Government Code (18 years old and a citizen of the state) and for any financial or District
policy conflicts of interests. After approval by staff, the Board will appoint the Successor
Officers through a Board Resolution. The Successor Officers will take an oath of office and will
stand ready to serve in the event of an emergency. Once seated on the Board following an
emergency, Standby Officers will be compensated on a per-meeting basis if they serve less than
one month, and will receive the same salary as a Regular Officer if they serve one month or
longer. Standby Officers will not be entitled to health or retirement benefits.

Several Board members have inquired if they may recommend and eventually appoint
immediate family members as Successor Officers. They reason that immediate family members are
easily recruitable, available, easiest to keep informed of the business of the District, and in the best
position to accurately represent the interests of the Regular Officer.

ANALYSIS

Section 87100 prohibits any public official from making, participating in making, or using
his or her position to influence a governmental decision in which the official has a financial
interest.² (Section 87103.) A conflict of interest may arise only when the reasonably foreseeable
financial effect of a governmental decision on a public official's interests is material. Interests from
which a conflict of interest may arise are defined in Section 87103, and include:

• An interest in a source of income to the official, including promised income, which
aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).)

• An interest in the official's personal finances, including those of the official's immediate
family. This is known as the "personal financial effects" rule. (Section 87103.)

² For purposes of decisions before an officials own agency, a public official "makes" a governmental decision
when the official, acting within the authority of his or her office or position, authorizes or directs any action, votes on a
matter, appoints a person, obligates or commits his or her agency to any course of action, or enters into any contractual
agreement on behalf of his or her agency. (Regulation 18704(a).) A public official "participates in making" a
governmental decision when he or she provides information, an opinion, or a recommendation in order to affect the
decision without significant intervening substantive review. (Regulation 18704(b).) A public official is attempting to
use his or her official position to "influence" a decision if, for the purpose of influencing, the official contacts or
appears before any member, officer, employee, or consultant of his or her agency. (Regulation 18704(c)(1).)

File No. A-17-045
Page No. 3

However, the Act excludes from the definition of "income" any "salary and reimbursement
for expenses or per diem, and social security, or other similar benefit payments received from a
state, local, or federal government agency . . ." (Section 82030(b)(2).) Accordingly, only the
official's interest in his or her personal finances is implicated by a decision to appoint an immediate
family member as a Successor Officer. As previously advised, a material financial effect on an
official's government salary may still be disqualifying under limited circumstances as a material
and foreseeable financial effect on the official's personal finances when the effect is distinct from
other officials. (See Cox Advice Letter, No. I-17-084 and Boydston Advice Letter, No. A-12-133.)

Generally, a financial effect is presumed to be reasonably foreseeable if the interest is a
named party in, or the subject of, a governmental decision before the official or the official's
agency. Foreseeability is presumed if the interest is "not explicitly involved" in the decision, a financial
effect is reasonably foreseeable if the effect can be recognized as a realistic possibility and more
than hypothetical or theoretical. A financial effect need not be likely to be considered reasonably
foreseeable. (Regulation 18701(b).)

Different standards apply to determine whether a reasonably foreseeable financial effect on
an interest will be material depending on the nature of the interest. (Regulation 18702.) For an
interest in the official's personal finances Regulation 18702.5 provide:

"(a) A personal financial effect means the financial effect of a governmental
decision on the personal finances of a public official or his or her immediate family.
The financial effect is material if the official or the official's immediate family
member will receive a measurable financial benefit or loss from the decision."

In this case, we must determine if the decision to appoint an immediate family member as a
Successor Officer will have a foreseeable and material effect. Because the interest is explicitly
involved in the decision, the effect is presumed foreseeable. Furthermore, if a Successor Officer is
seated, the Successor Officer will receive compensation equal to that of a Regular Officer.
Considering the potential compensation, the decision to appoint an immediate family member will
have a measurable financial benefit on the official and the potential effect is material. Accordingly,
an official may not make, participate in making, or use his or her position to influence a decision to
appoint an immediate family member as a Successor Officer.³

We note that you have specifically asked that we consider the application of Regulation
18702.5(b)(6), which provides that a personal financial effect does not include, "A decision to fill a
position on the body of which the official is a member." However, we find a significant distinction
between a decision to appoint an official or an immediate family member to the body and a decision
to merely select a sitting member of the body to a position on the body (such as filling a chair or
officer position on the board). Because we find that this provision was intended only to permit an

³ We note that conflict of interest provisions apply only to the related governmental official. Nothing would
preclude an immediate family member from expressing interest in a position as a Successor Officer or the District from
selecting an official's immediate family member as a Successor Officer so long as the related official does not make,
participate in making, or use his or her position to influence the decision. Additionally, the related official is not
disqualified from the decision if the immediate family member waives any income. (See Peat Advice Letter, No. I-05-
065.)

File No. A-17-045
Page No. 4

incumbent official to take part in decisions to appoint the official to a position on the body on which
the official already sits, this provision does not apply under the circumstances presented.

If you have other questions on this matter, please contact me at (916) 322-5660.

Sincerely,

Hyla P. Wagner
General Counsel

By:     Brian G. Lau
         Senior Counsel, Legal Division

BGL:jgl

May a member of the District's Board of Directors act to appoint an immediate family 
member as a Successor Officer under the California Emergency Services Act when the immediate 
family member will be compensated if seated as a Successor Officer following an emergency?

No. A member of the District's Board of Directors may not make, participate in making, or 
use his or her official position to influence any decision to appoint an immediate family member as 
a Successor Officer.

aggregates to $500 or more within 12 months prior to the decision. (Section 87103(c).)

when the official, acting within the authority of his or her office or position, authorizes or directs any action, votes on a

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: May a member of the District's Board of Directors act to appoint an immediate family 
member as a Successor Officer under the California Emergency Services Act when the immediate 
family member will be compensated if seated as a Successor Officer following an emergency?

CONCLUSION: No. A member of the District's Board of Directors may not make, participate in making, or 
use his or her official position to influence any decision to appoint an immediate family member as 
a Successor Officer.
